title: Lesson 16: IPC — Pipes, FIFOs, Shared Memory, Sockets

questions.question: Which IPC mechanism is the fastest because it avoids copying data between kernel and user space?
options: Unnamed pipes
FIFOs (named pipes)
Shared memory
Unix domain sockets
questions.explanation: Shared memory maps the same physical page into multiple processes' address spaces, eliminating the kernel-to-user copy that pipes, FIFOs, and sockets require.

questions.question: What is the key limitation of an unnamed pipe created with pipe()?
options: It can only transfer 4 KB of data total
It is unidirectional and limited to parent-child relationships
It requires root privileges to create
It cannot be used with fork()
questions.explanation: Unnamed pipes are unidirectional (data flows in one direction) and only work between related processes (parent and child after fork). FIFOs remove the parent-child restriction but are still unidirectional.

questions.question: Why does shared memory require explicit synchronization (e.g., semaphores) while pipes do not?
options: Shared memory is slower, so synchronization is needed for correctness
Pipes are managed by the kernel which handles synchronization internally; shared memory has no kernel mediation
Shared memory cannot be accessed by multiple processes simultaneously
Pipes automatically serialize all read/write operations without kernel involvement
questions.explanation: The kernel mediates all pipe reads and writes, providing implicit synchronization. Shared memory gives processes direct access to the same physical memory — the kernel is not involved in access, so processes must synchronize themselves.

questions.question: What unique capability do Unix domain sockets have that other IPC mechanisms lack?
options: They can only be used on Linux
They can pass file descriptors between processes via SCM_RIGHTS
They require no kernel involvement
They automatically serialize all data structures
questions.explanation: Unix domain sockets support ancillary data (cmsg) with SCM_RIGHTS, allowing one process to pass open file descriptors to another — something pipes, FIFOs, shared memory, and message queues cannot do.

questions.question: What is the fastest IPC mechanism on a single machine?
options: TCP sockets
Shared memory, because data doesn't need to be copied between processes
Pipes
Message queues
questions.explanation: Shared memory is the fastest IPC because both processes map the same physical pages into their address spaces. Data is shared without any copy — one process writes, the other reads directly. Synchronization (via semaphores or mutexes in the shared region) is still needed.
